title: Constitutional Law
subject: Constitutional Law
summary: Constitutional Law covers federal powers, federalism, due process, equal protection, religion, speech, and other individual rights.

sections.title: Big Picture
body: Constitutional Law questions usually require four steps:
Identify the government actor.
Identify the constitutional source of power or limitation.
Select the correct standard of review.
Determine the proper remedy or result.
The subject is easiest when divided into three large areas:
Federal powers and separation of powers
Federalism
Individual rights

blocks.text: Constitutional Law questions usually require four steps:
Identify the government actor.
Identify the constitutional source of power or limitation.
Select the correct standard of review.
Determine the proper remedy or result.
The subject is easiest when divided into three large areas:
Federal powers and separation of powers
Federalism
Individual rights
State-Action Requirement
Priority: Core
Rule: Most constitutional protections restrict government conduct, not purely private conduct.
State action may exist when
1. A private party performs a function traditionally and exclusively performed by government.
2. Government significantly encourages, commands, or participates in the conduct.
3. Government and the private party are heavily entangled.
Key distinctions
- Government licensing, regulation, or funding alone is generally insufficient.
Exam tip: Always identify the government actor before applying an individual-rights provision.
Equal Protection
Priority: Highest Priority
Rule: Equal protection analysis depends on the classification and the right affected.
Standards
- Strict scrutiny: race, national origin, and generally state alienage classifications. Law must be narrowly tailored to a compelling interest.
- Intermediate scrutiny: sex and nonmarital parentage. Law must be substantially related to an important interest.
- Rational basis: age, disability, wealth, and most other classifications. Law must be rationally related to a legitimate interest.
Key distinctions
- A facially neutral law generally requires discriminatory purpose plus discriminatory effect.
- Government bears the burden under strict and intermediate scrutiny.
- Challenger usually bears the burden under rational basis.
Exam tip: State the classification, scrutiny level, government interest, and fit.
Free Speech
Priority: Highest Priority
Rule: Speech analysis turns on whether the regulation is content based, viewpoint based, or content neutral and on the type of forum.
Key distinctions
- Content-based restrictions generally receive strict scrutiny.
- Viewpoint discrimination is almost always invalid.
- Content-neutral regulations generally receive intermediate scrutiny or time-place-manner review.
- Prior restraints are strongly disfavored.
Exam tip: Classify the regulation before analyzing whether it survives.
Time, Place, and Manner Restrictions
Priority: Highest Priority
Rule: In a traditional or designated public forum, a content-neutral restriction is valid when it is narrowly tailored to a significant government interest and leaves ample alternative channels.
Elements
1. Content neutral.
2. Narrowly tailored to a significant interest.
3. Ample alternative channels remain.
Key distinctions
- It need not be the least restrictive means.
- In a limited or nonpublic forum, the restriction generally must be reasonable and viewpoint neutral.
Exam tip: Determine the forum first.
Unprotected or Less-Protected Speech
Priority: Core
Incitement: Directed to producing imminent lawless action and likely to produce it.
True threats: Serious expression of intent to commit violence, with the constitutionally required culpable mental state.
Fighting words: Personally abusive face-to-face words likely to provoke immediate violence; narrowly applied.
Obscenity
1. Appeals to prurient interest under contemporary community standards.
2. Depicts defined sexual conduct in a patently offensive way.
3. Lacks serious literary, artistic, political, or scientific value.
Defamation: Public officials and public figures generally must prove actual malice, meaning knowledge of falsity or reckless disregard for truth.
Exam tip: Offensive or hateful speech remains protected unless it satisfies a defined exception.
Substantive Due Process
Priority: Core
Rule: Government infringement of a fundamental right receives strict scrutiny. Nonfundamental interests receive rational-basis review.
Fundamental rights commonly tested
- Marriage.
- Procreation.
- Contraception.
- Parental control over children’s upbringing.
- Interstate travel.
- Certain family-living arrangements.
- Refusal of unwanted medical treatment.
Exam tip: Carefully define the asserted right before deciding whether it is fundamental.
Dormant Commerce Clause
Priority: Highest Priority
Rule: States generally may not discriminate against or unduly burden interstate commerce.
Analysis
- Discriminatory law: generally invalid unless necessary to achieve an important nonprotectionist purpose and no reasonable nondiscriminatory alternative exists.
- Nondiscriminatory law: invalid only if the burden on interstate commerce is clearly excessive compared with local benefits.
Exceptions
- Congress authorizes the state law.
- State acts as a market participant rather than a regulator.
Exam tip: Ask whether the state is regulating the market or participating in it.
Congressional Commerce Power
Priority: Core
Rule: Congress may regulate:
1. Channels of interstate commerce.
2. Instrumentalities, persons, and things in interstate commerce.
3. Economic activities substantially affecting interstate commerce.
Key distinctions
- Economic activity may generally be aggregated.
- Noneconomic activity receives closer review.
Exam tip: Identify the regulated activity precisely before using aggregation.
State Sovereign Immunity
Priority: Highest Priority
Rule: Private parties generally cannot sue a nonconsenting state for damages in federal court.
Exceptions
- State consents.
- Congress validly abrogates immunity under Section 5 of the Fourteenth Amendment.
- Federal government or another state sues.
- Plaintiff seeks prospective injunctive relief against a state official under Ex parte Young.
Exam tip: Prospective relief against an official differs from retrospective payment from the state treasury.
Takings Clause
Priority: Highest Priority
Rule: Government may take private property for public use only with just compensation.
Types
- Permanent physical occupation: generally a per se taking.
- Total loss of all economically beneficial use: generally a taking, subject to background property-law principles.
- Partial regulatory taking: analyze economic impact, interference with reasonable investment-backed expectations, and character of government action.
- Exaction: requires essential nexus and rough proportionality.
Exam tip: A taking is not automatically unconstitutional; the ordinary remedy is compensation.
State Police Power
Priority: Additional
Rule: States possess general police power over health, safety, welfare, and morals. The federal government has only enumerated powers.
Exam tip: Traditional state regulation does not defeat a valid exercise of federal power.
Anti-Commandeering
Priority: Highest Priority
Rule: Congress may not require state legislatures or state executive officials to enact or administer a federal regulatory program.
Congress may instead
- Regulate private parties directly.
- Use federal officers.
- Preempt conflicting state law.
- Attach valid conditions to federal funds.
Spending conditions must be
- Clear.
- Related to the federal program.
- Independently constitutional.
- Not coercive.
Exam tip: Preemption is permitted; commanding the states to govern is not.

sections.title: 1. Constitutional Law Attack Plan
body: Ask:
Who acted—Congress, the president, a federal court, a state, or a private party?
What constitutional power authorizes the action?
Is another branch’s power being invaded?
Does federalism limit the action?
Is state action required?
What right or classification is affected?
What scrutiny applies?
Who bears the burden?
Does the law survive that scrutiny?
What remedy follows?
Memory line: Actor, power, limit, right, scrutiny, result.

sections.title: 2. Article III Judicial Power
body: PART I — FEDERAL JUDICIAL POWER
Article III creates one Supreme Court and allows Congress to establish lower federal courts.
Federal courts have only the jurisdiction authorized by:
Article III
Federal statutes enacted by Congress
Federal courts do not possess a general power to decide every legal dispute.

sections.title: 3. Cases and Controversies
body: Article III limits federal courts to actual cases and controversies.
Federal courts may hear matters involving:
Federal law
Treaties
Admiralty
The United States
States
Diverse citizens
Foreign governments and citizens
They may not issue advisory opinions.

sections.title: 4. Judicial Review
body: Federal courts may review the constitutionality of:
Congressional statutes
Executive actions
State statutes
State executive conduct
Final state-court judgments involving federal law
The Constitution is supreme, and the Supreme Court is the final judicial interpreter of federal constitutional law.

sections.title: 5. Scope of Injunctive Relief
body: Federal equitable relief generally should be no broader than necessary to provide complete relief to the parties before the court.
A court ordinarily should not issue universal relief merely because a federal policy applies nationwide.

blocks.text: Federal equitable relief generally should be no broader than necessary to provide complete relief to the parties before the court.

sections.title: 6. Eleventh Amendment and Sovereign Immunity
body: A private party generally may not sue a state in federal court without the state’s consent.
The immunity applies to suits by:
Citizens of another state
Foreign citizens
The state’s own citizens
It protects states and state agencies, but generally not cities or counties.

sections.title: 7. State Sovereign Immunity Outside Federal Court
body: States also generally retain immunity from private federal-law suits in:
Their own state courts
Courts of other states
The immunity is broader than the literal wording of the Eleventh Amendment.

blocks.text: States also generally retain immunity from private federal-law suits in:

sections.title: 8. State Consent and Waiver
body: A state may waive immunity.
Waiver must be clear.
A state may waive immunity by voluntarily removing an action from state court to federal court.

blocks.text: A state may waive immunity.
Waiver must be clear.

sections.title: 9. Ex parte Young
body: A federal court may enjoin a state officer from enforcing an unconstitutional state law.
The action must seek prospective relief against the officer, not retroactive damages from the state treasury.
Memory line: Sue the officer to stop the violation, not the state to collect past damages.

sections.title: 10. Individual-Capacity Damages
body: A state official may be sued personally for damages when the official acted unlawfully.
The judgment must be payable by the official rather than automatically from the state treasury.
Immunities such as qualified immunity may still apply.

blocks.text: A state official may be sued personally for damages when the official acted unlawfully.

Immunities such as qualified immunity may still apply.

sections.title: 11. Congressional Abrogation
body: Congress may abrogate state sovereign immunity when:
It speaks unmistakably clearly, and
It acts under a valid enforcement power, especially Section 5 of the Fourteenth Amendment
Congress generally may not abrogate state immunity using ordinary Article I powers.

blocks.text: Congress may abrogate state sovereign immunity when:

sections.title: 12. Structural Waiver
body: States surrendered immunity to certain suits in the constitutional structure.
Examples include some actions involving:
The federal government
Other states
Bankruptcy
Federal condemnation
Congress’s war powers

blocks.text: States surrendered immunity to certain suits in the constitutional structure.

sections.title: 13. Supreme Court Original Jurisdiction
body: The Supreme Court has original jurisdiction over cases involving:
Ambassadors and public ministers
States as parties
Congress cannot expand or contract the Supreme Court’s constitutionally assigned original jurisdiction.
Congress may give lower courts concurrent jurisdiction except in disputes exclusively between states.

blocks.text: The Supreme Court has original jurisdiction over cases involving:
Ambassadors and public ministers
States as parties
Congress cannot expand or contract the Supreme Court’s constitutionally assigned original jurisdiction.
Congress may give lower courts concurrent jurisdiction except in disputes exclusively between states.

sections.title: 14. Supreme Court Appellate Jurisdiction
body: Congress may regulate and make exceptions to the Supreme Court’s appellate jurisdiction.
Congress may not:
Dictate how a court must decide a particular pending case
Reopen a final Supreme Court judgment
Use jurisdiction stripping to violate another constitutional provision

sections.title: 15. Certiorari and Direct Appeal
body: Most Supreme Court review is discretionary through certiorari.
At least four justices must vote to grant review.
Direct mandatory appeals are rare and usually arise from specially constituted three-judge federal courts.

blocks.text: Most Supreme Court review is discretionary through certiorari.
At least four justices must vote to grant review.

sections.title: 16. Adequate and Independent State Grounds
body: The Supreme Court cannot review a state judgment resting on a state-law ground that is:
Adequate — independently resolves the case
Independent — does not depend on federal law
Otherwise, a ruling on federal law would be advisory.

sections.title: 17. Unclear State Grounds
body: When it is unclear whether a state court relied on federal or state law, the Supreme Court may review the federal issue.
A state court can avoid review by clearly stating that state law independently controls the outcome.

blocks.text: When it is unclear whether a state court relied on federal or state law, the Supreme Court may review the federal issue.

sections.title: 18. Standing
body: A plaintiff must establish:
Injury in fact
Causation
Redressability
Use ICR:
Injury
Causation
Redressability

Redressability

sections.title: 19. Injury in Fact
body: The injury must be:
Concrete
Particularized
Actual or imminent
A statutory violation alone is not enough unless it causes a concrete injury.
Widely shared injury may qualify if the plaintiff personally suffers it.

blocks.text: The injury must be:
Concrete
Particularized
Actual or imminent

Widely shared injury may qualify if the plaintiff personally suffers it.

sections.title: 20. Future Injury
body: A threatened injury must be certainly impending or present a substantial risk.
A merely speculative possibility of future harm is insufficient.
Injunctive relief requires a real likelihood that the plaintiff will suffer the harm again.

blocks.text: A threatened injury must be certainly impending or present a substantial risk.
A merely speculative possibility of future harm is insufficient.

sections.title: 21. Causation and Redressability
body: The injury must be fairly traceable to the defendant.
A favorable judgment must likely provide at least some meaningful relief.
Speculation about independent third parties may defeat causation or redressability.

blocks.text: The injury must be fairly traceable to the defendant.
A favorable judgment must likely provide at least some meaningful relief.
Speculation about independent third parties may defeat causation or redressability.

sections.title: 22. Generalized Grievances
body: A plaintiff generally may not sue merely as a concerned citizen who believes the government is violating the Constitution.
The plaintiff must show personal injury beyond disagreement with governmental conduct.

blocks.text: A plaintiff generally may not sue merely as a concerned citizen who believes the government is violating the Constitution.
The plaintiff must show personal injury beyond disagreement with governmental conduct.

sections.title: 23. Taxpayer Standing
body: Federal and state taxpayers generally lack standing to challenge government spending merely because they pay taxes.
A taxpayer always may challenge the taxpayer’s own tax liability.
A narrow Establishment Clause exception may apply to a specific legislative appropriation alleged to support religion.

blocks.text: Federal and state taxpayers generally lack standing to challenge government spending merely because they pay taxes.
A taxpayer always may challenge the taxpayer’s own tax liability.
A narrow Establishment Clause exception may apply to a specific legislative appropriation alleged to support religion.

sections.title: 24. Municipal Taxpayer Standing
body: A resident taxpayer may have standing to challenge unlawful municipal spending because the relationship between the taxpayer and local treasury is more direct.

blocks.text: A resident taxpayer may have standing to challenge unlawful municipal spending because the relationship between the taxpayer and local treasury is more direct.

sections.title: 25. Third-Party Standing
body: A plaintiff generally cannot assert another person’s rights.
Third-party standing may be allowed when:
The plaintiff has a close relationship with the rights holder
The rights holder faces a genuine obstacle to suing
The plaintiff has suffered an injury

sections.title: 26. Organizational Standing
body: An organization may sue on behalf of members when:
Members would have standing
The interests are germane to the organization’s purpose
Individual participation is generally unnecessary
The organization may also sue for its own direct injuries.

blocks.text: An organization may sue on behalf of members when:

sections.title: 27. Legislator Standing
body: A legislator ordinarily lacks standing merely because the legislator voted against a law and lost.
Standing is more likely when official votes have been completely nullified rather than merely politically defeated.

blocks.text: A legislator ordinarily lacks standing merely because the legislator voted against a law and lost.

sections.title: 28. Section 1983
body: Section 1983 provides a federal cause of action against a person acting under color of state law who violates federal rights.
It does not create substantive rights.
Proper defendants may include:
State and local officials in individual capacity
Municipalities under proper circumstances
Private actors functioning as state actors
States are generally not “persons” liable for damages under Section 1983.

sections.title: 29. Ripeness
body: A claim is unripe when brought too early.
Consider:
Fitness of the issues for judicial decision
Hardship from withholding review
A credible and imminent threat of enforcement often supports pre-enforcement review.

blocks.text: A claim is unripe when brought too early.

sections.title: 30. Mootness
body: A case is moot when no live controversy remains at every stage of litigation.
If the court can no longer provide effective relief, the case generally must be dismissed.

blocks.text: A case is moot when no live controversy remains at every stage of litigation.
If the court can no longer provide effective relief, the case generally must be dismissed.

sections.title: 31. Mootness Exceptions
body: A case may remain justiciable when:
The conduct is capable of repetition yet evading review
The defendant voluntarily ceased but could resume
Collateral consequences remain
A certified class retains live claims

blocks.text: A case may remain justiciable when:

sections.title: 32. Advisory Opinions
body: Federal courts cannot answer abstract legal questions or advise political branches.
A declaratory judgment is permitted only when the parties face a real, immediate dispute.

blocks.text: Federal courts cannot answer abstract legal questions or advise political branches.
A declaratory judgment is permitted only when the parties face a real, immediate dispute.

sections.title: 33. Political Questions
body: A federal court will not decide a matter when:
The Constitution textually commits it to another branch, or
No judicially manageable standards exist
Common examples include:
Impeachment trial procedures
Some foreign-affairs disputes
Partisan gerrymandering claims

sections.title: 34. Pullman Abstention
body: A federal court may abstain when a federal constitutional claim depends on an unsettled question of state law that state courts should resolve first.

blocks.text: A federal court may abstain when a federal constitutional claim depends on an unsettled question of state law that state courts should resolve first.

sections.title: 35. Younger Abstention
body: Federal courts generally will not enjoin a pending state criminal prosecution.
Exceptions include:
Bad faith
Harassment
A patently unconstitutional statute
No adequate opportunity to raise the federal claim

blocks.text: Federal courts generally will not enjoin a pending state criminal prosecution.

sections.title: 36. Burford and Colorado River Abstention
body: Burford: A federal court may abstain to avoid disrupting a complex state administrative scheme.
Colorado River: In exceptional circumstances, a federal court may defer to substantially parallel state litigation.

blocks.text: Burford: A federal court may abstain to avoid disrupting a complex state administrative scheme.

sections.title: 37. Enumerated Powers
body: PART II — CONGRESSIONAL POWER
Congress has no general police power.
Every federal statute must rest on:
An enumerated power
The Necessary and Proper Clause linked to an enumerated power
A Civil War Amendment enforcement power
Memory line: Congress needs a constitutional hook.

sections.title: 38. Commerce Clause Categories
body: Congress may regulate:
Channels of interstate commerce
Instrumentalities and persons or things in interstate commerce
Intrastate activities that substantially affect interstate commerce

blocks.text: Congress may regulate:

Instrumentalities and persons or things in interstate commerce
Intrastate activities that substantially affect interstate commerce

sections.title: 39. Channels of Commerce
body: Congress may regulate or protect:
Highways
Railways
Waterways
Air routes
Internet and communication channels
It may prohibit harmful items or conduct from moving through those channels.

blocks.text: Congress may regulate or protect:

Railways

It may prohibit harmful items or conduct from moving through those channels.

sections.title: 40. Instrumentalities of Commerce
body: Congress may regulate:
Vehicles
Aircraft
Trains
Ships
Persons and goods moving interstate
It may protect these instrumentalities even from intrastate threats.

blocks.text: Congress may regulate:

Aircraft
Trains

sections.title: 41. Economic Intrastate Activity
body: Congress may regulate intrastate economic activity when it rationally concludes that the activity, in the aggregate, substantially affects interstate commerce.
Small individual conduct may be aggregated when the regulated class is economic.
New example: Congress may regulate home production of a commodity when widespread home production would alter the national market for that commodity.

sections.title: 42. Noneconomic Intrastate Activity
body: Congress may not aggregate purely noneconomic conduct merely by describing an attenuated effect on commerce.
Relevant factors include:
Whether the conduct is economic
Whether the statute contains a jurisdictional interstate-commerce link
Congressional findings
The directness of the connection

sections.title: 43. Commerce Power Cannot Compel Commerce
body: The Commerce Clause allows regulation of existing activity.
It does not authorize Congress to force inactive individuals to enter a market.
Congress may sometimes reach the same conduct through taxation.

blocks.text: The Commerce Clause allows regulation of existing activity.

sections.title: 44. Taxing Power
body: A federal tax is generally valid when it:
Raises some revenue
Operates as a tax rather than a punitive criminal penalty
Does not violate another constitutional provision
Congress may tax for the general welfare.

blocks.text: A federal tax is generally valid when it:

Does not violate another constitutional provision
Congress may tax for the general welfare.

sections.title: 45. Direct and Indirect Taxes
body: Indirect taxes must be geographically uniform.
Direct taxes generally must be apportioned among the states.
Income taxes need not be apportioned because of the Sixteenth Amendment.

blocks.text: Indirect taxes must be geographically uniform.
Direct taxes generally must be apportioned among the states.
Income taxes need not be apportioned because of the Sixteenth Amendment.

sections.title: 46. Export Tax
body: Congress may not tax goods exported from the United States or activities closely tied to the export process.

blocks.text: Congress may not tax goods exported from the United States or activities closely tied to the export process.

sections.title: 47. Origination Clause
body: Bills for raising revenue must originate in the House of Representatives.
The Senate may amend them.
The rule applies to genuine revenue bills, not every measure that incidentally produces money.

blocks.text: Bills for raising revenue must originate in the House of Representatives.

sections.title: 48. Spending Power
body: Congress may spend for the general welfare even when it could not directly regulate the same subject.
Congress may attach conditions to federal funds.

blocks.text: Congress may spend for the general welfare even when it could not directly regulate the same subject.
Congress may attach conditions to federal funds.

sections.title: 49. Conditions on Federal Funding
body: A funding condition generally must be:
For the general welfare
Unambiguous
Related to the federal program
Consistent with other constitutional provisions
Not so coercive that the state has no genuine choice
Memory line: Clear, related, constitutional, not coercive.

sections.title: 50. Coercive Spending
body: Financial inducement becomes unconstitutional coercion when the threatened loss is so large that the state is effectively forced to comply.
A modest reduction in new funds is more likely valid than withdrawal of a massive existing program.

blocks.text: Financial inducement becomes unconstitutional coercion when the threatened loss is so large that the state is effectively forced to comply.

sections.title: 51. War and Defense Powers
body: Congress has broad authority to:
Declare war
Raise and fund armed forces
Regulate military forces
Establish military tribunals
Maintain national defense in wartime and peacetime

blocks.text: Congress has broad authority to:

sections.title: 52. Military Tribunals
body: Military courts may try:
Enemy combatants
Current service members
Certain enemy civilians
They generally may not try ordinary U.S. civilians when Article III courts are open.

blocks.text: Military courts may try:

sections.title: 53. Enemy Combatant Due Process
body: A U.S. citizen detained as an enemy combatant must receive a meaningful opportunity to challenge the factual basis for detention before a neutral decisionmaker.

blocks.text: A U.S. citizen detained as an enemy combatant must receive a meaningful opportunity to challenge the factual basis for detention before a neutral decisionmaker.

sections.title: 54. Habeas and Suspension
body: The privilege of habeas corpus may be suspended only in cases of rebellion or invasion when public safety requires it.
Persons held in territory under meaningful U.S. control may have constitutional habeas rights.

blocks.text: The privilege of habeas corpus may be suspended only in cases of rebellion or invasion when public safety requires it.

sections.title: 55. Congressional Investigations
body: Congress may investigate matters within a legitimate legislative sphere.
The investigation must relate to:
Existing laws
Proposed legislation
Government administration
Subjects on which Congress could legislate
Congress cannot conduct investigation solely to expose private wrongdoing or exercise ordinary law enforcement.

sections.title: 56. Congressional Subpoenas
body: A congressional subpoena must serve a valid legislative purpose and relate to a subject on which legislation could exist.
Witnesses retain constitutional protections, including the privilege against self-incrimination.

blocks.text: A congressional subpoena must serve a valid legislative purpose and relate to a subject on which legislation could exist.
Witnesses retain constitutional protections, including the privilege against self-incrimination.

sections.title: 57. Property and Postal Powers
body: Congress may regulate and dispose of federal property.
Congress may establish post offices and postal roads and impose reasonable mailing restrictions.
Neither power authorizes violation of other constitutional rights.

blocks.text: Congress may regulate and dispose of federal property.
Congress may establish post offices and postal roads and impose reasonable mailing restrictions.
Neither power authorizes violation of other constitutional rights.

sections.title: 58. Immigration and Naturalization
body: Congress has broad power over:
Admission of noncitizens
Removal
Naturalization
Conditions of citizenship acquired by statute
Noncitizens inside the United States receive due-process protection.
Congress generally cannot involuntarily strip a native-born citizen of citizenship.

blocks.text: Congress has broad power over:
Admission of noncitizens
Removal
Naturalization
Conditions of citizenship acquired by statute
Noncitizens inside the United States receive due-process protection.
Congress generally cannot involuntarily strip a native-born citizen of citizenship.

sections.title: 59. Elections Clause
body: States initially regulate the time, place, and manner of congressional elections.
Congress may alter those regulations.
States cannot add qualifications for members of Congress beyond those in the Constitution.

blocks.text: States initially regulate the time, place, and manner of congressional elections.
Congress may alter those regulations.
States cannot add qualifications for members of Congress beyond those in the Constitution.

sections.title: 60. Necessary and Proper Clause
body: The Necessary and Proper Clause allows Congress to choose rational means to carry out an enumerated federal power.
It is not an independent source of authority.
The means need not be absolutely necessary.

blocks.text: The Necessary and Proper Clause allows Congress to choose rational means to carry out an enumerated federal power.
It is not an independent source of authority.
The means need not be absolutely necessary.

sections.title: 61. Thirteenth Amendment Enforcement
body: Congress may regulate public and private conduct to eliminate:
Slavery
Involuntary servitude
Badges and incidents of slavery
This is the principal constitutional power allowing Congress to reach purely private racial discrimination.

blocks.text: Congress may regulate public and private conduct to eliminate:
Slavery
Involuntary servitude
Badges and incidents of slavery
This is the principal constitutional power allowing Congress to reach purely private racial discrimination.

sections.title: 62. Fourteenth Amendment Enforcement
body: Congress may enforce existing Fourteenth Amendment rights but may not redefine or expand them.
The remedy must be congruent and proportional to documented constitutional violations.
Congress may reach state action, not purely private conduct, under Section 5.

blocks.text: Congress may enforce existing Fourteenth Amendment rights but may not redefine or expand them.
The remedy must be congruent and proportional to documented constitutional violations.
Congress may reach state action, not purely private conduct, under Section 5.

sections.title: 63. Fifteenth Amendment Enforcement
body: Congress may enforce the prohibition against racial discrimination in voting.
Remedies burdening selected states must be justified by current conditions.

blocks.text: Congress may enforce the prohibition against racial discrimination in voting.
Remedies burdening selected states must be justified by current conditions.

sections.title: 64. Congressional Membership
body: Each chamber may judge member qualifications set by the Constitution.
Each chamber may punish members and expel a member with a two-thirds vote.
Neither Congress nor states may add new constitutional qualifications.

blocks.text: Each chamber may judge member qualifications set by the Constitution.
Each chamber may punish members and expel a member with a two-thirds vote.
Neither Congress nor states may add new constitutional qualifications.

sections.title: 65. Executive Power
body: PART III — PRESIDENTIAL POWER
The president executes federal law and manages the executive branch.
The president has no general power to make domestic law.
Presidential power is generally broader in foreign affairs than domestic affairs.

blocks.text: PART III — PRESIDENTIAL POWER

The president has no general power to make domestic law.
Presidential power is generally broader in foreign affairs than domestic affairs.

sections.title: 66. Pardon Power
body: The president may pardon federal offenses after they are committed.
The power includes:
Full pardons
Commutations
Reprieves
Remission of fines
Conditional pardons
Group amnesty
The president cannot pardon:
State crimes
Impeachment

blocks.text: The president may pardon federal offenses after they are committed.
The power includes:
Full pardons
Commutations
Reprieves
Remission of fines
Conditional pardons
Group amnesty
The president cannot pardon:
State crimes
Impeachment

sections.title: 67. Presentment and Veto
body: After Congress passes a bill, the president has 10 days, excluding Sundays, to:
Sign it
Veto and return it
Take no action
If Congress remains in session, inaction makes the bill law.
If Congress adjourns and prevents return, inaction produces a pocket veto.

blocks.text: After Congress passes a bill, the president has 10 days, excluding Sundays, to:
Sign it
Veto and return it
Take no action
If Congress remains in session, inaction makes the bill law.
If Congress adjourns and prevents return, inaction produces a pocket veto.

sections.title: 68. Line-Item Veto
body: The president may not cancel selected portions of a duly enacted statute.
Changing the text after presentment violates bicameralism and presentment.

blocks.text: The president may not cancel selected portions of a duly enacted statute.
Changing the text after presentment violates bicameralism and presentment.

sections.title: 69. Appointment Power
body: Principal federal officers are appointed by the president with Senate confirmation.
Congress may vest appointment of inferior officers in:
The president alone
Department heads
Federal courts
Congress itself may not appoint officers exercising executive authority.

blocks.text: Principal federal officers are appointed by the president with Senate confirmation.
Congress may vest appointment of inferior officers in:
The president alone
Department heads
Federal courts
Congress itself may not appoint officers exercising executive authority.

sections.title: 70. Removal Power
body: The president generally may remove executive officers.
Congress may sometimes require good cause for removal of:
Certain members of independent multimember bodies
Inferior officers with limited duties
Congress may not itself participate in removal outside impeachment.

blocks.text: The president generally may remove executive officers.
Congress may sometimes require good cause for removal of:
Certain members of independent multimember bodies
Inferior officers with limited duties
Congress may not itself participate in removal outside impeachment.

sections.title: 71. Youngstown Framework
body: Presidential power depends on Congress’s position:
Congress authorizes — presidential power is strongest
Congress is silent — uncertain zone of concurrent authority
Congress opposes — presidential power is weakest
Memory line: Yes, silence, no.

blocks.text: Presidential power depends on Congress’s position:

Congress is silent — uncertain zone of concurrent authority
Congress opposes — presidential power is weakest
Memory line: Yes, silence, no.

sections.title: 72. Take Care Clause
body: The president must faithfully execute valid federal laws.
The president may exercise enforcement discretion, but generally may not cancel or rewrite statutes.

blocks.text: The president must faithfully execute valid federal laws.
The president may exercise enforcement discretion, but generally may not cancel or rewrite statutes.

sections.title: 73. Commander-in-Chief
body: The president directs military operations.
Congress:
Declares war
Funds the military
Makes military rules
Many disputes about deployment are treated as political questions.

blocks.text: The president directs military operations.
Congress:
Declares war
Funds the military
Makes military rules
Many disputes about deployment are treated as political questions.

sections.title: 74. Treaties
body: The president negotiates treaties.
Ratification requires two-thirds of senators present.
Hierarchy:
Constitution
Later-in-time federal statute or treaty
Earlier federal statute or treaty
State law
A non-self-executing treaty requires implementing legislation.

blocks.text: The president negotiates treaties.
Ratification requires two-thirds of senators present.
Hierarchy:
Constitution
Later-in-time federal statute or treaty
Earlier federal statute or treaty
State law
A non-self-executing treaty requires implementing legislation.

sections.title: 75. Executive Agreements
body: The president may enter executive agreements without two-thirds Senate approval.
Executive agreements:
Prevail over conflicting state law
Do not prevail over conflicting federal statutes
Do not override the Constitution

blocks.text: The president may enter executive agreements without two-thirds Senate approval.
Executive agreements:
Prevail over conflicting state law
Do not prevail over conflicting federal statutes
Do not override the Constitution

sections.title: 76. Recognition Power
body: The president has exclusive power to recognize foreign governments.
Congress cannot require the executive branch to contradict that recognition decision.

blocks.text: The president has exclusive power to recognize foreign governments.
Congress cannot require the executive branch to contradict that recognition decision.

sections.title: 77. Impeachment
body: PART IV — SEPARATION OF POWERS
The House impeaches by majority vote.
The Senate convicts by two-thirds of senators present.
Grounds include:
Treason
Bribery
High crimes and misdemeanors
Impeachment does not prevent later criminal prosecution.

blocks.text: PART IV — SEPARATION OF POWERS

The Senate convicts by two-thirds of senators present.
Grounds include:
Treason
Bribery
High crimes and misdemeanors
Impeachment does not prevent later criminal prosecution.

sections.title: 78. Appropriations and Impoundment
body: Congress controls federal appropriations.
When Congress mandates that funds be spent, the president generally may not refuse to spend them.
Discretion exists when the statute grants discretion.

blocks.text: Congress controls federal appropriations.
When Congress mandates that funds be spent, the president generally may not refuse to spend them.
Discretion exists when the statute grants discretion.

sections.title: 79. Legislative Veto
body: Congress may not reverse executive action through a one-house or two-house resolution outside ordinary legislation.
Congress must use:
Bicameral passage
Presentment to the president

blocks.text: Congress may not reverse executive action through a one-house or two-house resolution outside ordinary legislation.
Congress must use:
Bicameral passage
Presentment to the president

sections.title: 80. Nondelegation Doctrine
body: Congress may delegate regulatory authority when it supplies an intelligible principle.
Almost all ordinary delegations survive.
Congress cannot delegate uniquely legislative powers such as impeachment.

blocks.text: Congress may delegate regulatory authority when it supplies an intelligible principle.
Almost all ordinary delegations survive.
Congress cannot delegate uniquely legislative powers such as impeachment.

sections.title: 81. Major Questions Doctrine
body: An agency needs clear congressional authorization to resolve a matter of vast economic and political significance.
General or ancillary statutory language is usually insufficient.

blocks.text: An agency needs clear congressional authorization to resolve a matter of vast economic and political significance.
General or ancillary statutory language is usually insufficient.

sections.title: 82. Congressional Control of Courts
body: Congress may change governing law and apply it to pending cases.
Congress may not:
Command a result under old law
Reopen final judgments
Exercise judicial power itself

blocks.text: Congress may change governing law and apply it to pending cases.
Congress may not:
Command a result under old law
Reopen final judgments
Exercise judicial power itself

sections.title: 83. Judicial Immunity
body: Judges have absolute immunity from damages for judicial acts unless they act in the clear absence of all jurisdiction.
The immunity does not cover nonjudicial administrative acts.

blocks.text: Judges have absolute immunity from damages for judicial acts unless they act in the clear absence of all jurisdiction.
The immunity does not cover nonjudicial administrative acts.

sections.title: 84. Legislative Immunity
body: Federal legislators are absolutely protected under the Speech or Debate Clause for legislative acts.
Protected conduct includes:
Floor debate
Committee work
Legislative reports
The protection does not cover:
Campaign speeches
Press releases
Bribery unrelated to proving legislative acts

blocks.text: Federal legislators are absolutely protected under the Speech or Debate Clause for legislative acts.

Committee work
Legislative reports
The protection does not cover:

Press releases
Bribery unrelated to proving legislative acts

sections.title: 85. Executive Privilege
body: Executive privilege protects confidential executive communications but is qualified.
The need for evidence in a criminal proceeding may overcome the privilege.
National-security information receives heightened protection.

blocks.text: Executive privilege protects confidential executive communications but is qualified.
The need for evidence in a criminal proceeding may overcome the privilege.
National-security information receives heightened protection.

sections.title: 86. Presidential Civil Immunity
body: A president has absolute civil-damages immunity for official acts.
The president has no immunity from civil litigation based on:
Pre-presidential conduct
Purely private conduct
Such litigation may proceed while the president is in office.

blocks.text: A president has absolute civil-damages immunity for official acts.
The president has no immunity from civil litigation based on:
Pre-presidential conduct
Purely private conduct
Such litigation may proceed while the president is in office.

sections.title: 87. Presidential Criminal Immunity
body: A former president has:
Absolute immunity for acts within exclusive constitutional authority
Presumptive immunity for other official acts
No immunity for unofficial acts
A president’s motives do not transform an official act into an unofficial act.

blocks.text: A former president has:
Absolute immunity for acts within exclusive constitutional authority
Presumptive immunity for other official acts
No immunity for unofficial acts
A president’s motives do not transform an official act into an unofficial act.

sections.title: 88. Presidential Subpoenas
body: A president is not categorically immune from federal or state criminal subpoenas.
Ordinary objections remain available, including:
Overbreadth
Bad faith
Harassment
Privilege
Congressional subpoenas for personal presidential information receive separation-of-powers scrutiny.

blocks.text: A president is not categorically immune from federal or state criminal subpoenas.
Ordinary objections remain available, including:

Bad faith
Harassment
Privilege
Congressional subpoenas for personal presidential information receive separation-of-powers scrutiny.

sections.title: 89. Qualified Immunity
body: Federal and state executive officials performing discretionary functions generally avoid damages unless they violate a clearly established federal right that a reasonable official would have known.
The standard is objective.

blocks.text: Federal and state executive officials performing discretionary functions generally avoid damages unless they violate a clearly established federal right that a reasonable official would have known.

sections.title: 90. Tenth Amendment
body: PART V — FEDERALISM
Powers not delegated to the federal government are reserved to the states or people.
The Tenth Amendment reinforces that the federal government has enumerated powers.

blocks.text: PART V — FEDERALISM
Powers not delegated to the federal government are reserved to the states or people.
The Tenth Amendment reinforces that the federal government has enumerated powers.

sections.title: 91. Anti-Commandeering
body: Congress generally may not command states to:
Enact federal regulatory programs
Administer federal laws
Force state officers to enforce federal policy
Congress may:
Regulate states directly
Attach valid funding conditions
Preempt contrary state laws

blocks.text: Congress generally may not command states to:

Administer federal laws
Force state officers to enforce federal policy
Congress may:
Regulate states directly
Attach valid funding conditions
Preempt contrary state laws

sections.title: 92. Generally Applicable Federal Laws
body: Congress may subject states to generally applicable federal regulation under an enumerated power.
Example: A federal wage law may apply to public and private employers alike.

blocks.text: Congress may subject states to generally applicable federal regulation under an enumerated power.
Example: A federal wage law may apply to public and private employers alike.

sections.title: 93. Federal Immunity from State Regulation
body: States may not regulate the federal government or discriminate against federal operations unless Congress permits it.
State law cannot control federal agencies in a manner inconsistent with federal policy.

blocks.text: States may not regulate the federal government or discriminate against federal operations unless Congress permits it.
State law cannot control federal agencies in a manner inconsistent with federal policy.

sections.title: 94. Federal Immunity from State Taxation
body: States may not directly tax:
The federal government
Federal instrumentalities
Federal property
States may impose nondiscriminatory taxes on private parties dealing with the federal government when the legal incidence does not fall on the federal government.

blocks.text: States may not directly tax:
The federal government
Federal instrumentalities
Federal property
States may impose nondiscriminatory taxes on private parties dealing with the federal government when the legal incidence does not fall on the federal government.

sections.title: 95. State Immunity from Federal Taxation
body: The federal government generally may tax state activities and employees through nondiscriminatory taxes.
A tax targeting states because they are states may violate federalism principles.

blocks.text: The federal government generally may tax state activities and employees through nondiscriminatory taxes.
A tax targeting states because they are states may violate federalism principles.

sections.title: 96. Dormant Commerce Clause
body: When Congress has not acted, state laws generally may not:
Discriminate against interstate commerce
Unduly burden interstate commerce
Deliberately regulate commerce wholly outside the state

blocks.text: When Congress has not acted, state laws generally may not:

Unduly burden interstate commerce
Deliberately regulate commerce wholly outside the state

sections.title: 97. Facial Discrimination Against Interstate Commerce
body: A law favoring in-state economic interests over out-of-state interests is usually invalid unless:
The state has a legitimate, nonprotectionist purpose
No reasonable nondiscriminatory alternative exists
This is an extremely demanding test.

blocks.text: A law favoring in-state economic interests over out-of-state interests is usually invalid unless:
The state has a legitimate, nonprotectionist purpose
No reasonable nondiscriminatory alternative exists
This is an extremely demanding test.

sections.title: 98. Protectionism
body: A state cannot protect local businesses from out-of-state competition merely to preserve jobs or local profits.
Economic protectionism is not a legitimate justification.

blocks.text: A state cannot protect local businesses from out-of-state competition merely to preserve jobs or local profits.
Economic protectionism is not a legitimate justification.

sections.title: 99. Market-Participant Exception
body: A state acting as a buyer or seller may favor its own citizens.
Examples:
Buying from local vendors
Selling state-owned goods to residents first
Favoring residents in a state-run business
The state may not impose downstream restrictions once it leaves the market in which it participates.

blocks.text: A state acting as a buyer or seller may favor its own citizens.

Selling state-owned goods to residents first

sections.title: 100. Traditional Government Function
body: A state may favor public governmental operations performing traditional functions, such as public waste processing.
The exception generally does not permit favoritism toward private local businesses.

blocks.text: A state may favor public governmental operations performing traditional functions, such as public waste processing.
The exception generally does not permit favoritism toward private local businesses.

sections.title: 101. Congressional Authorization
body: Congress may authorize state discrimination against interstate commerce.
The authorization must be unmistakably clear.

blocks.text: Congress may authorize state discrimination against interstate commerce.
The authorization must be unmistakably clear.

sections.title: 102. Nondiscriminatory Burdens
body: A neutral state law may still violate the Dormant Commerce Clause when the burden on interstate commerce is clearly excessive compared with local benefits.
This balancing is deferential and does not invite ordinary economic second-guessing.

blocks.text: A neutral state law may still violate the Dormant Commerce Clause when the burden on interstate commerce is clearly excessive compared with local benefits.
This balancing is deferential and does not invite ordinary economic second-guessing.

sections.title: 103. Extraterritorial Regulation
body: A state generally may not directly control commercial conduct occurring wholly outside its borders.
Ordinary laws with incidental out-of-state effects are not automatically invalid.

blocks.text: A state generally may not directly control commercial conduct occurring wholly outside its borders.
Ordinary laws with incidental out-of-state effects are not automatically invalid.

sections.title: 104. Complete Auto Tax Test
body: A state tax on interstate commerce is valid when it has:
Substantial nexus
Fair apportionment
Nondiscrimination
Fair relationship to state services
Use NAFR:
Nexus
Apportionment
Fairness to interstate commerce
Relation to services

Fair relationship to state services
Use NAFR:

Apportionment

sections.title: 105. Substantial Nexus
body: A physical presence is not required.
A substantial volume of economic activity directed into the state may create sufficient nexus.

blocks.text: A physical presence is not required.
A substantial volume of economic activity directed into the state may create sufficient nexus.

sections.title: 106. Apportionment
body: A state may tax only its fair share of interstate activity.
The tax should avoid:
Multiple taxation
Taxation of value earned elsewhere

blocks.text: A state may tax only its fair share of interstate activity.
The tax should avoid:

Taxation of value earned elsewhere

sections.title: 107. Sales and Use Taxes
body: A state may require a seller with substantial nexus to collect sales tax.
A complementary use tax on out-of-state purchases is valid when it does not exceed the in-state sales tax.

blocks.text: A state may require a seller with substantial nexus to collect sales tax.
A complementary use tax on out-of-state purchases is valid when it does not exceed the in-state sales tax.

sections.title: 108. Foreign Commerce Taxation
body: A state tax affecting foreign commerce must satisfy ordinary interstate-commerce requirements and must not:
Create a substantial risk of international multiple taxation
Prevent the nation from speaking with one voice

blocks.text: A state tax affecting foreign commerce must satisfy ordinary interstate-commerce requirements and must not:

sections.title: 109. Twenty-First Amendment
body: States have substantial authority over alcohol distribution.
That authority does not permit:
Discrimination against interstate commerce
Violation of free speech
Violation of equal protection
Establishment of religion

blocks.text: States have substantial authority over alcohol distribution.

Violation of free speech

sections.title: 110. Federal Preemption
body: Federal law preempts conflicting state law under the Supremacy Clause.
Preemption may be:
Express
Field
Conflict
Obstacle

blocks.text: Federal law preempts conflicting state law under the Supremacy Clause.
Preemption may be:
Express
Field
Conflict
Obstacle

sections.title: 111. Express Preemption
body: Congress may expressly state that state law is displaced.
Courts examine the statutory text and any savings clause.
Express preemption provisions are generally read according to their terms.

blocks.text: Congress may expressly state that state law is displaced.
Courts examine the statutory text and any savings clause.

sections.title: 112. Field Preemption
body: Federal regulation preempts the entire field when Congress intended exclusive federal occupation.
States cannot supplement even with consistent rules.

blocks.text: Federal regulation preempts the entire field when Congress intended exclusive federal occupation.
States cannot supplement even with consistent rules.

sections.title: 113. Impossibility Preemption
body: State law is preempted when simultaneous compliance with federal and state law is impossible.
A party generally must show genuine legal impossibility, not mere inconvenience.

blocks.text: State law is preempted when simultaneous compliance with federal and state law is impossible.

sections.title: 114. Obstacle Preemption
body: State law is preempted when it frustrates the purposes and objectives of federal law.
A legitimate state purpose does not prevent preemption.

blocks.text: State law is preempted when it frustrates the purposes and objectives of federal law.
A legitimate state purpose does not prevent preemption.

sections.title: 115. State Court Enforcement of Federal Rights
body: State courts generally must hear federal claims when they hear analogous state claims.
A state cannot discriminate against federal rights.
Congress may give federal courts exclusive jurisdiction over particular federal claims.

blocks.text: State courts generally must hear federal claims when they hear analogous state claims.
A state cannot discriminate against federal rights.
Congress may give federal courts exclusive jurisdiction over particular federal claims.

sections.title: 116. Interstate Compacts
body: States need congressional consent for an interstate compact when the agreement:
Enhances state power at federal expense
Affects federal authority
Alters the political balance
Not every interstate agreement requires consent.

blocks.text: States need congressional consent for an interstate compact when the agreement:
Enhances state power at federal expense
Affects federal authority
Alters the political balance
Not every interstate agreement requires consent.

sections.title: 117. Full Faith and Credit
body: A state must recognize a final sister-state judgment when:
The original court had jurisdiction
The judgment was on the merits
The judgment is final
A state has more flexibility regarding another state’s statutes than its judgments.

blocks.text: A state must recognize a final sister-state judgment when:

The judgment was on the merits
The judgment is final
A state has more flexibility regarding another state’s statutes than its judgments.

sections.title: 118. State-Action Requirement
body: PART VI — STATE ACTION
Most constitutional individual-rights provisions restrict government, not private conduct.
State action exists when private conduct is fairly attributable to the government.

blocks.text: PART VI — STATE ACTION
Most constitutional individual-rights provisions restrict government, not private conduct.
State action exists when private conduct is fairly attributable to the government.

sections.title: 119. Public Function
body: A private actor is a state actor when performing a function traditionally and exclusively performed by government.
Examples may include:
Conducting elections
Operating a company-owned town with full municipal powers
Providing ordinary public services is usually insufficient.

blocks.text: A private actor is a state actor when performing a function traditionally and exclusively performed by government.

Operating a company-owned town with full municipal powers
Providing ordinary public services is usually insufficient.

sections.title: 120. Significant State Involvement
body: State action may exist when government:
Commands discrimination
Jointly participates
Enforces a discriminatory arrangement through judicial process
Is deeply intertwined with private management
Mere regulation or funding ordinarily is insufficient.

blocks.text: State action may exist when government:

Jointly participates

Is deeply intertwined with private management
Mere regulation or funding ordinarily is insufficient.

sections.title: 121. Judicial Enforcement
body: A private discriminatory agreement becomes state action when a court enforces it.
The constitutional violation arises from judicial enforcement, not the private agreement standing alone.

blocks.text: A private discriminatory agreement becomes state action when a court enforces it.
The constitutional violation arises from judicial enforcement, not the private agreement standing alone.

sections.title: 122. Insufficient State Involvement

Business licenses
Public utility status
Heavy regulation
Government grants
Tax exemptions
Police protection

blocks.text: The following ordinarily do not alone create state action:

sections.title: 123. Procedural Due Process Framework
body: PART VII — PROCEDURAL DUE PROCESS
Ask:
Is there government action?
Is life, liberty, or property being deprived?
What process is due?
A bad decision does not itself violate procedural due process if adequate procedures were provided.

blocks.text: PART VII — PROCEDURAL DUE PROCESS

sections.title: 124. Property Interests
body: Property interests arise from an independent source such as:
Statute
Contract
Regulation
Established entitlement
A unilateral hope is not property.

blocks.text: Property interests arise from an independent source such as:

Contract
Regulation
Established entitlement
A unilateral hope is not property.

sections.title: 125. Liberty Interests
body: Liberty includes more than freedom from jail.
It may include:
Parental rights
Reputation plus loss of legal status
Freedom from bodily restraint
Certain occupational interests
Damage to reputation alone is ordinarily insufficient.

blocks.text: Liberty includes more than freedom from jail.

Reputation plus loss of legal status
Freedom from bodily restraint
Certain occupational interests

sections.title: 126. Mathews Balancing
body: The amount of process required depends on:
Private interest
Risk of erroneous deprivation and value of added safeguards
Government interest and administrative burden
Use PRG:
Private interest
Risk of error
Government burden

blocks.text: The amount of process required depends on:

Risk of erroneous deprivation and value of added safeguards
Government interest and administrative burden
Use PRG:

Risk of error
Government burden

sections.title: 127. Neutral Decisionmaker
body: Due process generally requires a neutral decisionmaker.
Disqualifying bias may arise from:
Direct financial interest
Personal animosity
Prior prosecutorial role
Extreme probability of actual bias

blocks.text: Due process generally requires a neutral decisionmaker.

Prior prosecutorial role
Extreme probability of actual bias

sections.title: 128. Notice and Opportunity to Be Heard
body: Due process usually requires:
Timely notice
Meaningful opportunity to respond
Hearing at a meaningful time and manner
A predeprivation hearing is favored when feasible.

blocks.text: Due process usually requires:

Meaningful opportunity to respond

sections.title: 129. Emergency Deprivations

Immediate action is necessary
Prior process is impracticable
Adequate later remedies exist

blocks.text: A postdeprivation hearing may suffice when:

sections.title: 130. Negligent and Random Conduct
body: Mere government negligence does not violate due process.
When a rogue official commits a random intentional property deprivation and the state cannot provide prior process, an adequate postdeprivation remedy may satisfy due process.

blocks.text: Mere government negligence does not violate due process.

sections.title: 131. Public Employment
body: A public employee with a protected entitlement generally must receive:
Notice of charges
Explanation of evidence
Opportunity to respond before termination
A fuller hearing may occur afterward.

Explanation of evidence
Opportunity to respond before termination

sections.title: 132. Public Education
body: A public-school student facing a short suspension generally must receive:
Notice
Explanation of evidence

Fundamental right → strict scrutiny
Nonfundamental liberty or economic right → rational basis

To achieve a compelling interest

Interest is unrelated to suppressing expression

blocks.text: Conduct receives speech protection when intended to convey a message likely to be understood.
A content-neutral conduct regulation is valid when:
Within government power
Furthers an important interest
Interest is unrelated to suppressing expression
Burden is no greater than necessary

sections.title: 210. Content-Based Regulation
body: A law is content based when application depends on:
Subject matter
Message
Idea
Communicative content
Content-based laws generally receive strict scrutiny.
Viewpoint discrimination is an especially forbidden form of content discrimination.

blocks.text: A law is content based when application depends on:
Subject matter
Message
Idea
Communicative content
Content-based laws generally receive strict scrutiny.
Viewpoint discrimination is an especially forbidden form of content discrimination.

sections.title: 211. Content-Neutral Regulation
body: A regulation is content neutral when justified without reference to the message.
It may receive intermediate scrutiny or forum-specific time, place, and manner review.

blocks.text: A regulation is content neutral when justified without reference to the message.
It may receive intermediate scrutiny or forum-specific time, place, and manner review.

sections.title: 212. Overbreadth
body: A law is facially invalid when it prohibits a substantial amount of protected speech compared with its legitimate scope.
A person whose own speech could be regulated may sometimes raise the rights of others.
The doctrine generally does not apply to commercial speech.

blocks.text: A law is facially invalid when it prohibits a substantial amount of protected speech compared with its legitimate scope.
A person whose own speech could be regulated may sometimes raise the rights of others.
The doctrine generally does not apply to commercial speech.

sections.title: 213. Vagueness
body: A law is void for vagueness when a person of ordinary intelligence cannot determine what is prohibited.
Vagueness creates:
Lack of fair notice
Arbitrary enforcement
Chilling of speech

blocks.text: A law is void for vagueness when a person of ordinary intelligence cannot determine what is prohibited.
Vagueness creates:
Lack of fair notice
Arbitrary enforcement
Chilling of speech

sections.title: 214. Prior Restraint
body: A prior restraint stops speech before it occurs.
It is strongly presumed invalid.
A licensing scheme must provide:
Narrow standards
Prompt decision
Prompt judicial review
Burden on government to justify suppression

blocks.text: A prior restraint stops speech before it occurs.
It is strongly presumed invalid.
A licensing scheme must provide:
Narrow standards
Prompt decision
Prompt judicial review
Burden on government to justify suppression

sections.title: 215. Unfettered Discretion
body: Officials may not receive standardless power to approve or deny speech permits.
A facial challenge may be brought without first applying under a system giving officials unfettered discretion.

blocks.text: Officials may not receive standardless power to approve or deny speech permits.
A facial challenge may be brought without first applying under a system giving officials unfettered discretion.

sections.title: 216. Compelled Speech
body: The First Amendment protects the right not to speak.
Government generally cannot compel a private speaker to:
Endorse an ideological message
Include an unwanted expressive participant
Create customized expression conveying a rejected viewpoint

blocks.text: The First Amendment protects the right not to speak.
Government generally cannot compel a private speaker to:
Endorse an ideological message
Include an unwanted expressive participant
Create customized expression conveying a rejected viewpoint

sections.title: 217. Compelled Financial Support
body: Government cannot compel a person to subsidize another private party’s political speech.
Examples include mandatory public-sector union agency fees.
Viewpoint-neutral student activity fees may be valid.

blocks.text: Government cannot compel a person to subsidize another private party’s political speech.
Examples include mandatory public-sector union agency fees.
Viewpoint-neutral student activity fees may be valid.

sections.title: 218. Government Speech
body: Government may choose the content of its own message.
The Free Speech Clause does not require viewpoint neutrality in government speech.
Government speech remains subject to other constitutional limits, including the Establishment Clause.

blocks.text: Government may choose the content of its own message.
The Free Speech Clause does not require viewpoint neutrality in government speech.
Government speech remains subject to other constitutional limits, including the Establishment Clause.

sections.title: 219. Identifying Government Speech
body: Consider:
History of the medium
Public perception
Degree of government control
Permanent public monuments and standard license plates are often government speech.

blocks.text: Consider:
History of the medium
Public perception
Degree of government control
Permanent public monuments and standard license plates are often government speech.

sections.title: 220. Government Employee Speech
body: A public employee receives protection when:
Speaking as a citizen
On a matter of public concern
The employee’s speech interest outweighs workplace-efficiency interests
Speech pursuant to official duties is generally unprotected.

blocks.text: A public employee receives protection when:
Speaking as a citizen
On a matter of public concern
The employee’s speech interest outweighs workplace-efficiency interests
Speech pursuant to official duties is generally unprotected.

sections.title: 221. Political Patronage
body: Government generally may not hire, fire, promote, or demote public employees because of political affiliation.
An exception exists when political loyalty is an appropriate requirement for the position.

blocks.text: Government generally may not hire, fire, promote, or demote public employees because of political affiliation.
An exception exists when political loyalty is an appropriate requirement for the position.

sections.title: 222. Campaign Contributions
body: Contribution limits receive a form of intermediate scrutiny.
They must be closely drawn to a sufficiently important anti-corruption interest.
Aggregate limits unrelated to quid pro quo corruption are highly suspect.

blocks.text: Contribution limits receive a form of intermediate scrutiny.
They must be closely drawn to a sufficiently important anti-corruption interest.
Aggregate limits unrelated to quid pro quo corruption are highly suspect.

sections.title: 223. Campaign Expenditures
body: Limits on independent campaign expenditures receive strict scrutiny.
Government generally may not limit:
A candidate’s own spending
Independent corporate spending
Independent union spending
Disclosure requirements may be valid.

blocks.text: Limits on independent campaign expenditures receive strict scrutiny.
Government generally may not limit:

Independent corporate spending
Independent union spending
Disclosure requirements may be valid.

sections.title: 224. Public Forums
body: Traditional public forums include:
Streets
Sidewalks
Parks
Designated public forums are spaces government intentionally opens for broad public expression.

blocks.text: Traditional public forums include:
Streets
Sidewalks
Parks
Designated public forums are spaces government intentionally opens for broad public expression.

sections.title: 225. Public-Forum Time, Place, and Manner
body: A content-neutral restriction is valid when it:
Is narrowly tailored
Serves a significant interest
Leaves ample alternative channels
It need not be the least restrictive means.

blocks.text: A content-neutral restriction is valid when it:
Is narrowly tailored
Serves a significant interest
Leaves ample alternative channels
It need not be the least restrictive means.

sections.title: 226. Limited and Nonpublic Forums
body: Government may reserve a nonpublic or limited forum for its intended use.
Restrictions must be:
Reasonable
Viewpoint neutral
Government may limit subject matter but cannot favor one side of an allowed subject.

blocks.text: Government may reserve a nonpublic or limited forum for its intended use.
Restrictions must be:
Reasonable
Viewpoint neutral
Government may limit subject matter but cannot favor one side of an allowed subject.

sections.title: 227. Private Property
body: The First Amendment generally provides no right to speak on another private person’s property.
A state constitution may provide broader rights.
Government cannot broadly prohibit ordinary speech on a person’s own private property.

blocks.text: The First Amendment generally provides no right to speak on another private person’s property.
A state constitution may provide broader rights.
Government cannot broadly prohibit ordinary speech on a person’s own private property.

sections.title: 228. Residential Speech
body: Government may prohibit targeted picketing focused on a single residence.
It may not broadly prohibit neighborhood marching or ordinary door-to-door advocacy.

blocks.text: Government may prohibit targeted picketing focused on a single residence.
It may not broadly prohibit neighborhood marching or ordinary door-to-door advocacy.

sections.title: 229. Injunctions Against Speech
body: A content-neutral injunction must burden no more speech than necessary to serve a significant interest.
A content-based injunction receives strict scrutiny.

blocks.text: A content-neutral injunction must burden no more speech than necessary to serve a significant interest.
A content-based injunction receives strict scrutiny.

sections.title: 230. Obscenity
body: Material is obscene under Miller when:
The average person applying contemporary community standards finds a prurient appeal
The work depicts sexual conduct in a patently offensive way defined by law
The work as a whole lacks serious literary, artistic, political, or scientific value
The third element uses a reasonable-person standard.

blocks.text: Material is obscene under Miller when:

The work depicts sexual conduct in a patently offensive way defined by law
The work as a whole lacks serious literary, artistic, political, or scientific value
The third element uses a reasonable-person standard.

sections.title: 231. Obscenity Rules
body: Government may prohibit:
Sale
Distribution
Public exhibition
Adults generally have a right to possess adult obscenity at home.
There is no right to receive or distribute it.

blocks.text: Government may prohibit:
Sale
Distribution
Public exhibition

There is no right to receive or distribute it.

sections.title: 232. Child Pornography
body: Government may prohibit possession and distribution of actual child pornography even if it is not obscene.
Virtual images using no actual children cannot automatically be prohibited as child pornography.
Offers presented as actual child pornography may be punished.

blocks.text: Government may prohibit possession and distribution of actual child pornography even if it is not obscene.
Virtual images using no actual children cannot automatically be prohibited as child pornography.
Offers presented as actual child pornography may be punished.

sections.title: 233. Incitement
body: Advocacy may be punished only when it is:
Intended to produce imminent lawless action
Likely to produce imminent lawless action
Abstract endorsement of illegality is protected.

blocks.text: Advocacy may be punished only when it is:
Intended to produce imminent lawless action
Likely to produce imminent lawless action
Abstract endorsement of illegality is protected.

sections.title: 234. Fighting Words
body: Fighting words are personally abusive words likely to provoke an immediate violent response.
Most modern fighting-words laws fail because they are:
Vague
Overbroad
Viewpoint discriminatory

blocks.text: Fighting words are personally abusive words likely to provoke an immediate violent response.
Most modern fighting-words laws fail because they are:

Overbroad

sections.title: 235. True Threats
body: True threats are unprotected.
The government must prove at least recklessness regarding whether the statement would be understood as a serious threat of violence.

blocks.text: True threats are unprotected.
The government must prove at least recklessness regarding whether the statement would be understood as a serious threat of violence.

sections.title: 236. Defamation and Public Officials
body: A public official or public figure must prove actual malice:
Knowledge of falsity, or
Reckless disregard of truth
The plaintiff must prove falsity when constitutional protections apply.

blocks.text: A public official or public figure must prove actual malice:

Reckless disregard of truth
The plaintiff must prove falsity when constitutional protections apply.

sections.title: 237. Private Figures and Public Concern
body: A private plaintiff suing over speech on a matter of public concern must prove at least negligence to recover actual damages.
Presumed or punitive damages generally require actual malice.

blocks.text: A private plaintiff suing over speech on a matter of public concern must prove at least negligence to recover actual damages.
Presumed or punitive damages generally require actual malice.

sections.title: 238. Commercial Speech
body: Truthful, nonmisleading speech proposing lawful transactions receives intermediate protection.
A restriction is valid when:
Government interest is substantial
Regulation directly advances it
There is a reasonable fit
False or unlawful commercial speech may be prohibited.

blocks.text: Truthful, nonmisleading speech proposing lawful transactions receives intermediate protection.
A restriction is valid when:
Government interest is substantial
Regulation directly advances it
There is a reasonable fit
False or unlawful commercial speech may be prohibited.

sections.title: 239. Charitable Solicitation
body: Charitable solicitation receives strong speech protection.
Government may regulate fraud and require neutral financial disclosure.
It generally may not impose arbitrary percentage limits on fundraising costs.

blocks.text: Charitable solicitation receives strong speech protection.
Government may regulate fraud and require neutral financial disclosure.
It generally may not impose arbitrary percentage limits on fundraising costs.

sections.title: 240. Freedom of the Press
body: The press has no greater general constitutional rights than the public.
The press may be subject to ordinary laws such as:
Trespass
Taxation
Subpoena rules
Employment laws

blocks.text: The press has no greater general constitutional rights than the public.
The press may be subject to ordinary laws such as:
Trespass
Taxation
Subpoena rules
Employment laws

sections.title: 241. Gag Orders
body: An order preventing the press from publishing information is a prior restraint and is almost always invalid.
Courts should use alternatives such as:
Change of venue
Voir dire
Continuance
Jury instructions
Restrictions on trial participants

blocks.text: An order preventing the press from publishing information is a prior restraint and is almost always invalid.

Voir dire
Continuance
Jury instructions
Restrictions on trial participants

sections.title: 242. Access to Criminal Proceedings
body: The public and press generally have a right to attend criminal trials and jury selection.
Closure requires:
Overriding interest
Narrow tailoring
Consideration of alternatives
Specific findings

blocks.text: The public and press generally have a right to attend criminal trials and jury selection.
Closure requires:
Overriding interest
Narrow tailoring
Consideration of alternatives
Specific findings

sections.title: 243. Publication of Lawfully Obtained Information
body: Government generally may not punish the press for publishing truthful, lawfully obtained information about a matter of public concern.
The result may differ when the publisher participated in unlawful acquisition.

blocks.text: Government generally may not punish the press for publishing truthful, lawfully obtained information about a matter of public concern.
The result may differ when the publisher participated in unlawful acquisition.

sections.title: 244. Broadcast, Cable, and Internet
body: Broadcast: receives greater regulation because of historical scarcity
Cable: intermediate level of protection
Internet: receives full First Amendment protection; content regulation generally receives strict scrutiny

blocks.text: Broadcast: receives greater regulation because of historical scarcity
Cable: intermediate level of protection
Internet: receives full First Amendment protection; content regulation generally receives strict scrutiny

sections.title: 245. Freedom of Association
body: PART XV — ASSOCIATION
The First Amendment protects association for:
Political
Religious
Expressive
Ideological purposes
Government interference generally requires a compelling justification when it seriously burdens expressive association.

blocks.text: PART XV — ASSOCIATION

Religious
Expressive
Ideological purposes
