sections.title: 246. Expressive Association
body: A group may exclude a person when forced inclusion would significantly affect the group’s ability to advocate its message.
Ordinary commercial clubs receive less protection when exclusion is unrelated to expression.

blocks.text: A group may exclude a person when forced inclusion would significantly affect the group’s ability to advocate its message.
Ordinary commercial clubs receive less protection when exclusion is unrelated to expression.

sections.title: 247. Membership Liability
body: Membership in an organization may not be criminalized unless:
The group engages in illegal advocacy or conduct
The defendant knows of the unlawful aims
The defendant specifically intends to further them

blocks.text: Membership in an organization may not be criminalized unless:

sections.title: 248. Loyalty Oaths
body: Government may require an oath to support the Constitution and oppose unlawful overthrow.
An oath is invalid when vague, overbroad, or punishing mere association without knowing intent.

blocks.text: Government may require an oath to support the Constitution and oppose unlawful overthrow.
An oath is invalid when vague, overbroad, or punishing mere association without knowing intent.

sections.title: 249. Political Parties and Primaries
body: Political parties possess associational rights in selecting nominees and participants.
States may impose reasonable election regulations but cannot force a party to associate in ways that seriously alter its message or nominee-selection process.

blocks.text: Political parties possess associational rights in selecting nominees and participants.

sections.title: 251. Federal Power Comparison
body: Government Actor — Governing Principle
Congress — Must rely on enumerated or enforcement power
President — Executes law; power depends heavily on Congress’s position
Federal courts — Must have jurisdiction and an Article III case or controversy
States — Possess general police power but are limited by federal supremacy and individual rights

sections.title: 252. Commerce Comparison
body: Doctrine — Core Question
Congressional Commerce Clause — Is Congress regulating channels, instrumentalities, or substantially affecting activity?
Dormant Commerce Clause — Is a state discriminating against or unduly burdening interstate commerce?
Preemption — Has valid federal law displaced state law?
Article IV Privileges and Immunities — Is a state discriminating against nonresident citizens regarding fundamental activities?

sections.title: 253. Due Process vs. Equal Protection
body: Situation — Primary Doctrine
Right denied to everyone — Substantive due process
Right denied to a specific group — Equal protection
Government takes liberty or property without adequate procedures — Procedural due process
Government regulates property so severely it becomes a taking — Takings Clause

body: Forum — Rule

Limited public forum — Reasonable and viewpoint neutral within forum limits
Nonpublic forum — Reasonable and viewpoint neutral
Private property — No federal speech right against owner
Government speech — Government may select its own message

sections.title: 255. Final MBE Traps
body: Congress has no general police power.
The Necessary and Proper Clause is not independent.
Economic intrastate activity may be aggregated; noneconomic activity usually may not.
Spending conditions must be clear and not coercive.
A president cannot make domestic law merely by executive order.
Congress cannot use a legislative veto.
Congress may not commandeer state legislatures or officers.
Dormant Commerce Clause market participation does not excuse Article IV discrimination.
Federal law may preempt even complementary state law.
Constitutional rights generally require state action.
Disparate impact alone does not trigger heightened equal-protection scrutiny.
Race receives strict scrutiny even when the government says the preference is beneficial.
Sex receives intermediate scrutiny with an exceedingly persuasive justification.
Poverty is not generally suspect.
Abortion is not currently recognized as a federal substantive due-process right.
A permanent physical occupation is a per se taking.
Bills of attainder and ex post facto rules apply to punitive legislation.
Neutral generally applicable laws usually do not violate free exercise.
Excluding religion from neutral public benefits may violate free exercise.
Content-based speech regulation usually receives strict scrutiny.
Viewpoint discrimination is almost always invalid.
A law can fail for overbreadth, vagueness, prior restraint, or unfettered discretion.
Contribution limits and expenditure limits use different standards.
The press has no general immunity from laws applying to everyone.

sections.title: Final One-Sentence Summary
body: Constitutional Law asks whether the correct government actor used a valid constitutional power, respected federalism and separation of powers, and satisfied the scrutiny required by the individual right or classification involved.
